preamble: The shift from the voiceless bilabial stop [p] to its voiced counterpart [b] is a classic example of voicing lenition. It most frequently occurs in intervocalic positions where the stop adopts the voicing of the surrounding vowels, effectively 'relaxing' the vocal cords to maintain vibration throughout the word.

examples.note: Latin intervocalic [p] voices to [b] in Spanish.
Latin [p] before [r] voices to [b] in Spanish.
French historical voicing [p] > [b], then further lenition to [v].
Intervocalic voicing: single voiceless stops are realized as voiced when between vowels (e.g., /p/ → [b]).
Intervocalic voicing: /p/ is realized as [b] between voiced sounds (e.g., 'bibimbap' [pibimdap]).

sources: Penny: A History of the Spanish Language
